We just want to keep the friction at the front of the funnel as smooth or as frictionless as possible. And then from there, we're typically going to move our product into presenting a self-liquidating offer. And that's going to be, again, typically a low ticket offer. We're not going to charge too much for it, excuse me, because again, we want to be mindful of that friction and the objective in these particular steps is not to actually make money but to convert a prospect into a customer. And we know that once they're a customer, of course, they're going to be 10 times at least more likely to purchase whatever it is we want to sell them. And there are plenty of advantages to going offline now. The reason is that most people have forgotten about it. Most people aren't concerned about it. Most advertisers don't spend much time looking at their offline options because they are convinced rightly so to some respect that the internet has taken over offline media. Now while I respect that the internet is super important and without it I wouldn't be able to run my businesses or my clients businesses for that matter. Even the clients that are real offline businesses, they still have a very strong and significant online presence and that's trend is only moving upwards. Okay, so the internet is super important. But what's happened with everybody floating offline, everybody leaving or fleeing the offline platforms or media is that it's created a vacuum. It's created a real opportunity for us to buy some really cheap ad space, some really cheap media. And we're not going to be silly enough to buy some cheap media and just put our phone number there and expect the whole process to go offline because we know better than that offline to offline is a challenge. It is a real challenge and that's why a lot of the advertisers are leaving the offline platforms. They're leaving all the offline media because they've realized that if I put a newspaper ad encouraging people to get on the phone or encouraging people to mail me an envelope, tell me or return an envelope to me and have their credit card details in there and all that kind of thing, it becomes a real challenge. I mean, your conversion rates are frankly horrendous, very difficult to profit from a campaign like that. But what they don't do is run hybrid campaigns, which are campaigns that are specifically designed to generate the lead or begin the conversation with your prospect offline, but end the conversation all the way to sale through an online sales funnel. And we're going to talk about exactly how we can go about doing that and take advantage of this opportunity left by a lot of the advertisers since abandoning offline media. So let's talk about the offline lead gen mechanisms that we can play with. All right, there are a few that are not included there and I purposely not included them there for a few reasons. One that's not there. That's particularly important is telephone sales or telemarketing sales. And the reason that's not there is because I'll talk about telemarketing sales or phone sales once we really get to online to offline as opposed to offline to online. The reason being, I've never seen it work to any amount of success. I've seen it tried plenty of times, but I haven't seen it work where a company would hire or outsource a telemarketing team. A telemarketing team is just a team of salespeople over the phone. And the sole objective of that particular sales team is to convert the prospect to a buyer by directing him or her to an online page, which would be the sales funnel page. I haven't seen it work. I've seen people try it. So I've seen telemarketers in particular campaigns that are accompanies, excuse me, that I've consulted with who would run campaigns and they would call a particular buyer or prospect and try to sell them say health insurance or life insurance. And when once the buyer says they're not interested, will the buyer ask for more information rather than deflecting that objection and looping as we're taught to do in the straight line persuasion system. These particular sales team were instructed by whoever was managing their campaign at the time to actually direct them to a website. Tell if you want more information, here's a website and they would give them the website and the website will be the first page of the sales funnel, which at first glance seemed like a good idea because you haven't actually abandoned the sale and you might have thought the sale was dead. So rather than just leaving it, you do have the opportunity or you are granted the opportunity for one last shot and you can try to put them through an online sales funnel. While it may sound like it works or it might sound logical, in practice it failed and it failed over and over again. I haven't seen any success with respect to an offline to online sales funnel that actually began with or the conversation started with a cold phone call. So that's why I haven't included it there. Although you could absolutely construct a sales funnel that way and I've seen it constructed that way, but I haven't seen it constructed that way with any amount of success. The first thing we need to do is write perfect ad. Now you guys all understand the importance and significance of copywriting. We understand the importance and significance of establishing the three tens and building up positives and knocking out negatives and making sure that the product of the prospect, excuse me, is totally in love with our product, likes and trusts us and likes and trusts our company. But the space ad being as small as it is, accomplishing the three tens, passing the action threshold, building up the positives, knocking out the negative, it becomes next to impossible. So here's our objective for the space ad. We need to write a headline and the headline needs to accomplish one thing and one thing only. And that's to get the reader's attention. You had noticed guys just how many ads there were on that classified page. There were countless, many, many ads. Your ad needs to stand out. It needs to stand above the pack. People need to be directed to your ad right away since they get on the page. And that is the job of your headline. So like Jordan says, you need to demonstrate to your prospect in the first 30 seconds that you're an expert in your field and you're a force to be reckoned with. This is in the straight line persuasion training. Similarly, here we don't have quite three or four seconds, but in the first second or the first couple of seconds, the reader needs to be sucked in. They need to, and by sucked in or hooked in, I mean that as though they need to really engage in our message. Okay. So the reader really needs to want to read the rest of the particular ad. And it's only going to be a small ad, right? So we don't have too much copy in this particular case. So we're going to have a headline and the headline is going to attract as much interest as possible. Okay. Then we're going to have some body copy and the body copy is just going to be a little bit about our product. If we have enough space and the ads large enough, there's no harm in selling ourselves and our company too. Although most of that will be done in our sales funnel, but we really do want the prospect to want to read the ad, read to the ad, gets really excited about whatever it is we're speaking about. That's the job of the body copy. So interest in the headline, excitement in the body copy. They really need to start getting excited and really like our product or want to know more at least about our product or service. And then three is our CTA, which is our core to action. And our core to action is of course the domain name or the URL or the website address or the first page in our sales funnel. Now those of you who are using say, click funnels or lead pages or any of the other self hosted platforms will know that you're actually given a really long convoluted URL. And that's not the end of the world when you're talking about banner ads, for example, right? Because someone's going to see a banner ad, you're going to click that banner ad, then they're going to go to a really difficult complex URL and they're going to land on your landing page. And there's no big deal. That's not an issue because they didn't even, they didn't even go through that process. They didn't even register the fact that the landing page was, excuse me, the URL or so long convoluted. It didn't matter to them. They didn't need to type it into their URL box. It wasn't a direct URL insertion. Doesn't matter. They just clicked an ad and they went on to your landing page and it didn't really matter how long and confusing the actual landing page address was. In this case, it matters and it really matters. If you send traffic with a CTA to a domain page that does something like www.davidsbusiness slash click funnels slash 10362985, I can promise you conversion to a B zero. They'll be zero. And if you do get one conversion, you must have written the best ad in history because it's just asking too much from the prospect. We need to simplify the sales process. So look at these 7 million weekly circulation, 200 plus cities going across 200 cities in the United States for $639. It's amazing. It really is. And let's get on to it right away. All right, guys, so let's look at the next way of advertising in a newspaper, of course, with the primary objective of going from offline, which in this case is our newspaper advertisement to online, which is, of course, our pre-built perfect SLM sales funnel. And that is one of my favorite ways of actually doing this. It's a little sneaky, but it's all ethical and it's really cool and it works like Game Busters. And that is, of course, the advertorial. So let's talk about an advertorial. All right, guys, I don't expect you to be able to read all of the copy on this particular advertorial. So I'll actually include it in the resource area for you guys to have a look. This was an advertorial written by perhaps the greatest copywriter ever, a gentleman who's since passed named Gary Halbert. And he put this together. Now, here's the thing about an advertorial. An advertorial is actually, it is not an advertisement as much as it is a news piece. So it is absolutely an advertisement in that we've actually paid to have this ad run in a newspaper or in a magazine. But the objective is it is expected to be native or it's supposed to be a native type of advertisement, meaning it's expected to take on the form and function of the regular content of that newspaper or magazine. So in other words, it's an advertisement that looks a lot like a news article. So in this particular case, the prehead is need some fast cash. Try this with an elix and then the headline is the amazing money, money making secret of a desperate nerd from a higher, you know, very, very, um, attention grabbing headline. He uses the, you know, there, a lot of contrast. And the actual, this actual mail piece was wildly successful, incredibly successful. It made a whole lot of money and generated a whole bunch of leads and sales. And it's actually one of the most successful pieces of, um, advertorial, um, content ever released, ever published, um, ever circulated. And it's very old though. Okay. So it's, it won't work today. I don't believe it will work today, but it worked perfectly well to the market. Um, when it was actually published and circulated, but you can see the objective of this particular advertorial is to sell a product. Okay. So at the end, Gary, Helbert has a CTA and it goes like this. Here's how to order. All you have to do is write up your name, address, uh, and the word maximum money on a piece of paper and send it with a check, a check or money order in the amount of 1995 plus postage and handling, uh, and the totals of $22.95 and to that particular address. So this is way before the internet rights. So this is before the, if Gary was around today, I don't doubt he would have said, um, simply go to WW and whatever. But so in this particular case, they actually expected the prospect to fill out a form, send it back to head office with a check, and that would have functioned as the self liquidating offer. In your case, you guys won't be doing that, but you're more likely to not be sending them to a opt-in page. Uh, if you're doing a small little advert, like the one we saw, um, in the last example, in this particular case, it is possible, it is absolutely possible for you to send them straight to your self liquidating offer straight to a low cost item, uh, because you're actually going to be spending that, you know, significantly more money to actually run these half page ads than you would with the small little classified ads. So that is what an advertorial is. It is an advertisement that looks a lot like a newspaper article or a magazine article, it's expected to follow, excuse me, the form and function of the, uh, magazine or newspaper that it's been placed in, and it really sells your product, it gives you an opportunity because it's much larger. It gives you an opportunity to sell your product really well, to sell yourself really well, and to sell your company really well, effectively establishing the three 10s in the actual news piece itself, you know, because you're actually expected to write it as though you yourself were a newspaper reporter and you were interviewing this particular person or you were assessing this, a particular supplement and you were just crazy about the supplement, crazy about the company, crazy about the, you know, the genius mind behind it and you wrote up a great article. So that's the kind of form and function that an advertorial, advertorial, advertorial, excuse me, follows. I want you guys to read this advertorial about a thousand times because it was such a wildly successful advertorial. And, uh, so for in the net, find a few more if you're, if you're interested or while you're reading the next newspaper, see if you can spot the advertorials from the real articles without cheating. Of course, an advertorial can't run unless it has, depends of course, and whereabouts in the world you are, but in most countries and, um, uh, and most publishers will actually require you to have a little stamp in this particular case, the stamps on the right hand side says the word advertisement. More times than not, there's actually an equally sized stamp right in the center on the top above the headline that says paid advertisement or something along those lines. So that's what a reader, excuse me, expect a reader is expected to be able to tell the difference between an article published by the actual newspaper magazine from an actual piece of advertorial based on that little stamp that's put on the top of the article. But have a read the, the main purpose of this and what I'm trying to impress upon you guys is that an advertorial is simply an advertisement, but an advertisement that looks like a newspaper and functions a lot like a newspaper except it has a CTA. Okay. A typical newspaper article will not have a CTA. They won't ask visitors or readers to do anything. They'll just list some content, some fascinating content that a visitor is likely to be, or excuse me, that a reader is likely to be interested in, in this particular case. We also have the content, you know, with a, with a, um, attention grabbing headline and so forth. The rules hasn't changed, haven't changed. Excuse me, the straight line marketing, straight line principles, straight line persuasion principles haven't changed, but we're going to have a CTA or a call to action close to the end of this newspaper article or this advertorial that encourages the reader to go on to a particular website. And of course, the URL of that website will be the first or second page of our sales funnel. You're either going to put them into an opt-in page if you really want to increase conversions and not really afraid of falling into the red at the front end, you know, not profiting from the front end, or you can send them straight to a self-liquidating offer and hopefully break even at the front end and then make all your money in the back end of your funnel. All right, guys, so provided you have a kick-ass piece of content, meaning you've written an advertorial and it works really well, so the headline is capturing, is captivating and captures the audience. It really grabs their attention. So that's it for the newspaper section. So let's move on to direct mail. All right, guys, let's talk about direct mail. Now, there's only two direct mail was at one point hot. It was probably the hottest medium for direct response marketers available at one particular point in time, many years ago, prior to the internet. Now, though, I want you guys to approach direct mail with caution. Now, direct mail is especially useful in B2B spaces, and I'll show you how we can use it to really get through the gatekeeper and make contact with business owners who are our prospects if we're selling business to business, which of course is what B2B is. And it can be used for postcards for B2C. You know, I do believe in postcards as well. But the days of sending a package, a big direct mail package where it's got a sales letter, a series of postcards, maybe a little book as well in there. And you're really trying to elicit a sale or offline through an expensive package. And it costs a lot of money to send this stuff out. They are I think those days are behind us. I think they're long gone. You can still use the old school direct mail packages and send them to clients, but they have to be clients. You know, you have to have sold to them already. You can't use them for acquisition. You can use them in your back end. OK, so you can use them in the back end of your business. For instance, because I spend a lot of money with Google, Google sent me this postcard, but it was a postcard with little few bits and pieces in it. It was like a heavy lumpy type postcard. And the reason they were able to do that, one, because they're one of the largest companies in the world and they have that marketing budget, but certainly because they're giving it to somebody who is already a trusted and a valued customer or client of theirs. They wouldn't be doing it to people who have never done business with before, because it's just too expensive. It's hit and miss and it's not the most ideal acquisition strategy. And what we're talking about now, guys, is acquisition. We're focused on growing and growing fast and capturing as much market share as possible. Right. This is what the focus of this course is on or about. So with that in mind, with our acquisition goggles on, we're all concerned about right now is trying to attract as many clients as possible and put them through a sales funnel. There's only two direct mail pieces or direct mail strategies that I recommend for you guys. And depending on what kind of business you're in, you'll lean to one or the other. All right, so let's take a look at them. All right, so there are only two particular strategies. The first is 3D mails. And I'll explain to you what a 3D mail is, how to get it started and why it works so well and when you should use it. And I'll do the same for postcards. That's it. OK, I don't want you guys sending brochures out. I don't want you guys sending sales letters out in people's mailbox. I mean, it just doesn't work anymore. You know, and I'm not telling you never to do it. I'm not. I'm just telling you not to do it to people who aren't your customers already. If they're already your customers and you have a list and you have their physical address and you want to send them a piece, a package that has, you know, all these goodies in there, even if you're you can sell welcome baskets to new clients and that welcome basket can, you know, have another postcard inside it that instructs them to go to go to go online, excuse me, to a particular URL where they can download something for free and you can try to sell them something else after they've just become your client, provided their high value. All those opportunities exist and you're only limited by your creativity. But in terms of acquisition, in terms of acquiring new customers, in terms of trying to grow our market share with respect to the actual number of people in our market, as opposed to the actual revenue dollars, we're not talking about upselling existing customers right now. We're not talking about the back end. We're talking about the front end getting people into our funnel. And as long as that's how concerned as it is now, then there's only two particular direct mail strategy strategies I want you guys to focus on. And they are 3D mails and 3D mail, excuse me, and postcards. Let's look at the first. All right. So 3D mail is a pretty cool. It's a pretty cool strategy. OK, it's all about putting a grabber or a piece, something. They call it a grabber in direct marketing speak, but it's just anything inside the envelope, inside the mail, so that it serves a few purposes. One, it's big and lumpy. And if the mail itself is lumpy, people are most likely to read it. OK, so just imagine yourself if you've got an envelope and it's actually large, you know, there's something you can tell there's something physical inside there. You're going to want to read it, right? It's probably going to be the first piece of mail you open. So that serves for that purpose. So it grabs their attention. The next option is because it's so lumpy, it can't actually sit at the bottom of a pile, right? Because it's because it's oddly shaped. It's oddly shaped so it can't sit at the bottom of the pile of your envelopes. It has to sit right at the top just because by virtue of the fact that it has an awkward on shape, it has to be at the top of the pile, which means it's going to get read first. And the next benefit is, especially if you're in B2B or selling business to business, so your customers or clients or prospects are all business owners. Then what you're going to want to do is definitely take advantage of 3D mail because typically the greatest issue in trying to get hold of a decision maker in a B2B sale is getting through the gatekeeper. The gatekeeper is typically the secretary or the PA or the legion of managers and God, whoever else is guarding the executive or decision maker. And we need to try to circumnavigate that and it can be overwhelming. But if you actually have a lumpy piece of mail, so mail with some object that's clearly in it, more often than not, the secretary or the PA or whoever it is that manages the flow of mail from reception to the executive's office or decision maker's office will likely just pass it straight to the decision maker and not open it and not look at whether or not it's junk mail or a sales material or whatever because they think it's personal. They think it's private. A lot of the time, secretaries are uncomfortable opening lumpy mail because it ostensibly is something that's very private and something that's especially sent out to the executive and not sales material at all. And mind you, when we say gatekeeper, the gatekeeper's primary job is just to reject sales pieces. That's it. Almost everything goes through to the executive unless there's a piece of administration that can be passed on to another worker. Almost everything will go through to the decision maker outside of sales pieces. So we don't want out mail to look like sales pieces if we're in the B2B space and one of the coolest and most innovative ways and the most effective ways to do that. If you're in business to business is 3D mail. Now, great resource. If you're interested in running 3D mail campaigns is 3dmailresults.com. In that particular website, you're going to find a whole bunch of sample 3D mail sales letters and you're going to find all these little grabbers and pieces you can throw in the envelope and all that's left to do, of course, is to put the object inside the envelope along with the sales, put the address of your particular prospect and send it out. Stamp on it, of course, and send it out. It's that simple. Now, I recommend this particular process for B2B only for a few reasons. One, because the advantages like some of the ones we just mentioned, especially with respect to being able to circumnavigate or get around the gatekeeper. The other reason I recommend this particular strategy for B2B is because it's not cheap, right? It costs a lot of money to buy a grabber. Throw it into an envelope, pay for the postage, and because it's oddly shaped, the postage is going to cost you a little bit more money as well. So that costs adds up. So it's okay, though, if it costs a little bit of money, it's okay if the customer is worth a whole lot to you. That's why we build sales funnel, right? We want to increase the value of every one of our customers. Now, the same old is true here. It's okay for us to purchase so much, to spend so much money on acquiring a customer if the customer is worth a whole lot to us. And typically, the most valuable customers, if we're talking about one customer, one customer worth to us, the most valuable customers are going to come by way of B2B transactions, not B2C transactions. B2C is not to say you can't be successful. You can be ultra successful in B2C, but you're playing a volume game, right? You want lots and lots of clients, whereas B2B, you don't need so many clients because each client is worth so much more to you than they would be if it was a B2C deal. But they are, of course, more expensive to acquire. So, say you run a direct mail campaign, and the campaign costs you $2,000, okay? So, you sent out a direct mail piece there to 150 people, 200 people, and you spent $1,000, okay? And of that, you converted it at 1%. And that 1% conversion translated to about one or two sales, right? So, you spent $2,000, and you've only gotten two sales. But because the B2B deal is so... The B2B customer or client has or possesses such high value to you as a business owner, then what would happen is, although you've only got the one client and you spent $2,000 to acquire them, they bought a piece of software. For example, you have a software for restaurant owners, right? And that piece of software automates the restaurant sales process and tells people whether or not the restaurant is full and manages the marketing for the restaurant tour and manages their stock and their order and inventory and so on and so forth. It's a piece of software. But that software costs $5,000 a year, $5,000 over 12 months. So, it doesn't really matter that you spent $2,000 to acquire that customer because provided that customer stays with you for six months, which you would know your numbers, you would know the average retention of every single one of your clients. What did they say with your six months? They've already given you $2,500 and you've spent $2,000 on them. So, even that six month period, you're still on the $500 profit and that's only from the one campaign. So, you can see why it works for B2B. It makes sense, the math stacks up, right? But for B2C, it doesn't, okay? Because your client's not gonna be worth $5,000 to you. I don't know what you're selling, but you couldn't possibly be making $5,000 margin per sale, you know, if you're selling B2C. I mean, B2C, we're talking about clothes, even cars. Cars don't have much of a margin, right? So, most of the, most car dealerships are actually going under right now and they're in a whole lot of trouble only because the margin is so razor thin and they're actually making their money through the insurance, through all the fit out or through the add-ons, the insurance, the carpet on the floor, the whatever, you know, they'll continue to tell you all the add-ons that you should have with your car and it's those add-ons that are actually making the money. So, that's the sales funnel for them. They're actually encouraging you to buy a car as cheaply as possible. They try to make it as little as possible and that for them is their self-liquidating offer and then they do an upsell where they try to sell you the floor mats and insurance and whatever it is else that they've cited upsell you wanted and that is where they're making their margin, right? So, even though it's a very high-ticket product, still it's B to C and B to C more often than not is very low customer value with respect to the margin. Okay, so that's the case. And just while we're on that, another example that just came to mind at Petrol stations, those of you who are familiar with the business, I'm very familiar with it. We have a few clients in that space and Petrol stations don't actually make money from the sale of petrol. They make next to nothing. That's why petrol stations now are often in a war against each other and by petrol stations, for those of you who are not in Australia or England or the UK, petrol station is gas station, right? Gas station. A gas station doesn't make too much money from the sale of gas or the sale of petrol and where they will make most of their money is of course from the shop attached to the gas station. So, you'll find gas stations often in competition with each other and you get very aggressive and they're trying to lower the price and lower the price and some of them may even be prepared to take a hit on the petrol or on the gas to prepare to lose on the gas because the gas will act as a self-liquidating offer, right? And the people can come in, they can spend and the gas station owners are prepared to break even or even lose money on that at the front end of their funnel. They provided that at the back end of their funnel, they start making good sales at the shop and that is going to provide them all of their margin. All right, so that was a long-winded way of saying that mail was 3D mail is actually an ideal platform or media for business to business campaigns but I don't recommend it for business to consumer campaigns because the margins will be too much to bear. All right, so let's talk about examples of 3D mail. So, this is like a little grabber that's going to be inside the 3D mail and you can buy all of these from 3Dresults.com. Okay, so this is one of the examples, it's a bank bag and then you would try to tie it in. So, you would try to tie your sales letter because with this bag there will be a sales letter that you're gonna mail to the business owner. Say, in an envelope, for example, you can try to put it in the bag but I wouldn't because it'll probably get caught by the gatekeeper because they're gonna know it's like a novelty item. So, typically you wanna put it in an envelope and then put the actual sales letter along with the bank bag in the envelope and then you would have to tie in the letter so you don't seem like an idiot having just sent an empty bank bag. So, you're tying it in with something like warning, only read this letter if you want to fill this bag with money and you're selling some kind of an opportunity to increase that business owner's sales or train their sales team or run their marketing campaigns or so on or so forth. Now, just be mindful, approach these with caution because some of them are really cheesy, you know? They're a little bit corny but they work. They work, I promise you they do work but some of them are a little corny. So, here you go, another restaurant business owner, you would put this little plastic garbage bin or rubbish bin, however you would pronounce it and you would put that into an envelope along with the sales letter and the sales letter. The title of the sales letter, the headline will be something like, are you throwing away sales in your restaurant business? Again, you need to tie in the grabber to whatever it is you're trying to sell to them. So, you do that in the sales letter and it will be in the headline. So, now the business owner knows why there's a garbage bin in the little envelope. Okay, another example, that this message in a bottle can map out the hidden profits in your business guaranteed. Do you notice all the titles, all the headlines guys are all B2B based? You know, do you think that's a coincidence? Of course not, you know, the B2B is the only way I would recommend you guys moving forward with 3D mail. I don't again recommend moving forward with 3D mail if you are going to be running B2C or business to consume a campaign. So, this message in the bottle can map out the hidden profits in your business guaranteed. Okay, and then you got that message in the bottle and you can send it out. All right, here's the next one. You won't be handcuffed with our industry leading guarantee. You know, if you're an insurance company or if you know, you're a bank or whatever business you're in, frankly, if you have a strong solid guarantee, then you know, that might be a good idea. You can throw those handcuffs in there that make the envelope lumpy and when the business owner opens up the envelope or the sales letter, they will immediately have captured their attention. Okay, and the last one I wanna show you guys is this one which is a pill bottle and the headline of the sales letter will be something like just what the doctor ordered and then you will go on to list the benefits of your product and talk about you as a, whatever it is that you're delivering and talk also about your company and make sure you accomplish the three 10s, pass through the action threshold, stack up the positives, knock out the negatives and then I'm convinced you guys will be able to close anybody who is closeable. All right, so let's talk about the next method. The next method is postcards. No need to spend too much time on this particular one but this is just the direct mail alternative for B2C guys, all right? So if you're in the business to consumer space, again, if they're already your customers and you already have a customer list, you can spend a little bit more money on them and you can start doing, you know, lumpy mail if you want, or you can start doing more detailed packages and send that through to them through the mail and that's fine, you know, it works, it's fine, it's absolutely fine because the repeat customer is worth a whole lot more to you than a new customer, so you can definitely afford to spend a little bit more money on them. But we're talking about acquisition now and I wouldn't do that in acquisition or cost too much, there is very little chance you'll actually profit from a campaign like that but you can certainly profit from a campaign like this. Now this is a series of postcards, now we only got the one example here, no need to spend too much time on it. I don't like this one only because I don't see any really compelling offer, they've got a big discount there, 20% off any single item. I like that, you know, that would work, that would get some people interested, but what I don't like especially about it is outside of only being a 20% off, you know, so you're still going to have to buy something, I would much rather them have offered something for free completely, even if it's a digital product, but 20% off or 15% or 20, 30% off, so wide or whatever, with no catches is also a really good one, I mean, I wouldn't sneeze at it, I think it would convert, but what I don't like about it is the little copy at the end which says, present this certificate valid for in-store use only, so they've expected to hold on to this postcard for the next time that they're prepared to go to Bed Bath and Beyond, only God knows when they're actually gonna go to Bed Bath and Beyond next, they're supposed to save this postcode, excuse me, and they're supposed to be left in mint condition, I don't know where they expect, where the good folks at Bed Bath and Beyond expect the consumer to be keeping this postcard because it can't fit in your wallet, you know, so I don't know what they're expecting us to do, maybe put it in your car and leave it there until it's Bed Bath and Beyond time, they're asking for far too much from the customer or client, and they're forcing them offline to offline, they're not letting them go offline to online, which I don't like, just don't think it works, we need the customer to take immediate action, we want them to see our postcard and go straight online, straight to our URL, no stops, we want them to direct path from the postcard, straight to our URL, and that URL, of course, is going to be the opt-in page for the first page of our sales funnel, and we want to offer them something really compelling on the postcard, it's super, super compelling, that encourages them to go straight online without doing anything in between, because believe me, if they've forgotten about you for a second, they've forgotten about you forever, if they don't take immediate action, they will take no action, just remember that in marketing, okay, if they're not going to act on the spot, they're not going to act at all, so you can do a similar offer to this, I don't mind the 20% off, you know, it's not the end of the world, I'd rather it for a beef or free, but if you really want to profit on the front end, or at least break even on the front end, you may want to offer a self-liquidating offer, say a 20% off, you know, a product or a really cheap product, you know, a $7 bath kit, you know, a bath and shampoo kit or whatever, and encourage them to put that $7, and take advantage of that $7 deal, go online right away, free postage and handling $7, they get that bed bath kit, they're going to go to the order form, purchase the bed bath kit, sorry, the bath and shower kit, whatever it is, open shower, excuse me, whatever, you know, whatever items they sell over there at bed bath and beyond, we don't have that over here, that I imagine that's what's being sold there, so they want to make that purchase right from the beginning, and then that will be the self-liquidating offer, and then you want to make it, after they've given us the credit card details, we want to do an immediate upsell, one click sale for a more expensive item at bed bath and beyond, but something really compelling that they have to buy now, because if they don't buy now, they won't be able to get it, even if they go to one of the stores, it has to be purchased now, and then a series of optional upsells and downsells, and obviously put that customer in your list so they can be marketed to in the future. All right, so that's how we do it, that's how we will do it for B2C, that's the most effective and efficient way to do it for B2C, and 3DMails is the single most effective way to do it for B2B, and just like I argued that 3DMail wouldn't work for B2C, I argue equally as strongly that postcards won't work for B2B, and the reason they won't work for B2B is quite obvious, they don't have any of the benefits that will require for B2B, remember that in B2B we wanted them, wanted it to be able to go through the gatekeeper, this ain't going through the gatekeeper, gatekeeper's gonna see this advertisement, and it's gonna go straight in the garbage bin, it's not gonna make anywhere near the decision maker, not in a million years, that is clearly an advertisement, the gatekeeper is not interested in passing this advertisement on, no matter how compelling the ad is, no matter how much a percentage the director is being offered off of your service, so if we stick with the restaurant software analogy, if you're prepared to give the executive 95% off a full restaurant software package, which means that they'll pay next to nothing and everybody else in the market is paying thousands and thousands, ridiculously generous offer, he still won't see it, there's no offer that's good enough to make it pass the gatekeeper, so then be mindful of that, that's why that won't work for B2B, and also we said that it's going to grab the attention, it's gonna sit at the top of the stack of work emails, or sorry, work letters or envelopes, because it was oddly shaped, so you couldn't sit at the bottom because all the other mail would slide off it, so we're gonna get that lumpy mail, one's gonna sit right at the top, in this particular case, it's so flat that if it doesn't make it to the bin right away or make it to the bottom of the stack and no one's ever gonna read it, so B2B, 3D mail, if you're B2C don't think about it, please, don't even try it, especially at the acquisition stage, and for B2C, postcards is the way to go, and if you're B2B, please don't waste your money on postcards, they won't work, no matter how cheaply you can distribute them, they won't result in any sales, all right? All right guys, let's talk about our last offline media, at least with respect to our offline, so online campaigns, and that's TV and radio, so there's two pretty cool ways to use both of these media, let's go through them, all right? So with radio and TV, you have an option to do what, most of you guys I'm sure are pretty unfamiliar with, and that's a pretty interesting call strategy that not many people know and not many people talk about, and that's the broadcast strategy, and we apply SLM principles, and we can run a really cool broadcast strategy campaign, and we'll talk about it in a moment, and then there is the traditional direct marketing approach with respect to radio and TV, and that's what you guys are most familiar with, that's what you guys see when you turn on the TV, that's the kind of thing you guys hear when you turn on the radio, only the actual advertisements are probably what needs a little bit of work, so obviously if we're running a radio campaign, needs to capture the listener's attention, then it needs to run through the three 10s, they make sure the listener loves us already, loves our product, likes and trusts us, likes and trusts our company, and then they need to be given a simple URL, just like we've done, or we explored in both our newspaper and direct mail approaches and strategies, they need to be given a nice simple URL, and they have to go to that URL, which they will then be put into our sales funnel, which is our perfect sales funnel, of course. So that's pretty basic, and we don't need to spend too much time on that, although I'll give you guys a few little tips, but what's most interesting is the broadcast strategy because not many people know it, and it can work really, really well. So let's talk about broadcast. All right, so our broadcast strategy is a broken radio, so that's pay to play, which means that you guys at any point in time can buy your own radio show, you can go to a radio network, and then you can buy a show, and you can be the host of that show, and that comes with a huge amount of credibility. You can start inviting high-powered guests to attend the show. If you're in B2B, you would have, say, a group of 100 clients called the Dream 100 list, which was made popular by a really great marketer who's passed away named Chet Holmes, and he suggested that every single B2B player, or everybody who is selling to businesses, ought to have a list of 100 clients that they really, really want to work with. Okay, so that 100 clients, and you need to work at that list every single day, or every week, or every month, but you need to work at that 100 over and over and over again. So even if that person, even if one of the clients or prospects on that 100 list said, no, you need to come back to them a week later, or a month later, or six months later, and so forth, and that's how a Dream 100 list works. And broken radio is a real clever way of actually developing a relationship with some of the people on that list. If you have your own Dream 100 list, a list of 100 people you really want to work with, or a list of 100 prospects you really want to have turned into your customers, and you end up creating your own radio channel, or excuse me, Radio Show, and you're the host of that radio show, and you then invite that particular person, somebody who was on that list, to come in as a special guest, and believe me, almost everybody is prepared to say yes, or at least consider getting on a radio show, because they're thinking exposure, they're thinking that it's a great platform for them to sell their ideas, or it's even very flattering to be invited in the first place, so it's a great way for building relationships, connecting with people, and it is an excellent strategy in the B2B space. I've seen it work 100 times, and here's the thing, it doesn't matter if people aren't listening. If you actually have a huge audience, and you actually turn into a celebrity, fantastic, you know, you can leverage that fame and make a lot of money out of it, but it doesn't even need to be the case. People don't need to listen to it. The fact that you have your own radio station, or radio show, excuse me, really lifts your credibility, really lifts your authority. You really do become a force to be reckoned with, which of course is a key principle in the straight line persuasion system, and this is one way of just becoming a force right away, them knowing that you're the real deal. If you approach a client, even if it's on the phone, and you're able to begin the conversation with, you know, I'm the host of a radio show, I'd like to speak to whoever, you're very likely to get right past the gatekeeper and speak to the decision maker right away, so it's an awesome little strategy. Not many people know how to do it, and I'll talk you guys through it very quickly. All right, so with the broken radio, it is pay to play, so you don't need to be selected, all you need to do is write a check and pass an audition. So you write a check, you give it to a particular network, and you'll typically elect either a weekly half hour show, or a weekly one hour show, and then you'll be inviting guests, and you'll be speaking to people, and so on and so forth. You'll typically be required to submit a demo, or formally apply, you know, so apply for the spot, and that typically involves talking to somebody, talking about what your show's going to be, maybe you bring along a tape, or having recorded an interview with somebody else, demonstrate some interview skills, it's not nearly as difficult as you think, to get on the show, it's actually very easy, because they want so many people to broadcast as possible, because of course that is a secondary revenue stream for the network. Now, it's your choice, if you're nervous speaking live, and you think you don't want to go into a studio and record live, and people are listening, and you could make a mistake, and well that's not important at all, you can do a pre-recorded show. Same rules apply, you know, you'll just make sure that throughout the show, you're doing a good job, you know, selling yourself, and selling the company, your company, or at least bringing it up, and at least also bringing your product to what it is you do for a living, right? And then of course you'll be interviewing people, especially keep an eye on people who you really want to work with, if you're in B2B space, and there's some real high-valued customers or prospects, really keep an eye on them, and keep inviting them to the show if you decide to take this broadcast strategy route. Now, what's cool about it also, is you get some commercial slots, okay? So as part of your deal, you'll get a package, and you also get some commercial slots, and commercial slots are exactly what they sound like, it is a simple commercial on the network, so you get regular normal radio commercials. Okay, so that's the broadcast strategy, and you know all the value that can come out of having your own radio show, especially in the B2B space, even if it's in the B2C space, you can still think about the idea, but you really have to know your costs per lead, and you really have to know how much each customer is worth to you to see whether or not it makes sense for you to host your own radio show, and whether or not the credibility, the authority, the ability to navigate around gatekeepers, excuse me, is really worth the actual costs of getting on a show or hosting your own show. So you need to make up your mind in that respect, and weigh up, just to do a little bit of maths, and weigh up the positives, and see whether or not it makes sense to you, but for B2B players, it really is a clever way to get a hold of the big players in your space who you really want to work with, and who would normally not even consider taking your phone call, or responding to any of your direct mail pieces. So think about that broadcast strategy, and this is how you would go about it. There are a lot of different options, this one option is from Salem Media Group, you can just go to Salemmedia.com, and you can see up there on the navigation bar, the fourth item there is broadcast with us, you will just click that and follow the prompts, and see whether or not it makes sense for you guys to broadcast and run some of your promotional or marketing efforts through your own radio show, a radio show, and the good thing about that is, of course, you're going to be in a position of authority and power, and you really will be positioned in a way in which you are indeed a force to be reckoned with, which is a key cornerstone of the straight line principal, or the straight line persuasion principal's excuse me. All right, so now let's talk about traditional direct marketing approach. Now, before the traditional direct marketing approach, there really isn't too much for us to discuss outside of everything we've discussed in the course, you know exactly what should be in your ad, right? Guys, you know you need to get their attention, you know you need to have some compelling body copy, you know you need to have an awesome offer to get them to the beginning of your funnel, and you also know you need to have a nice simple URL for them to remember, because they're going to be hearing you on the radio, or watching you on TV, or watching your actors on TV, or listening to your voice over talent on the radio, and they're really just going to have to commit that particular URL to memory, okay? So they're not going to go on that second, they may need to park the car, they may need to pull their phone out and do whatever it is they have to do in order to get onto the sales page, or into the opt-in page, so the URL needs to be easy, all right? So that's the first thing. And the second thing is, so you guys know how to write a script, that's all about writing the script, right? You guys are script experts, you have a lot of Jordan's written scripts, Jordan wrote those scripts in the Copywriting Module himself, and he spent hours and hours and hours writing them, right? And you guys know a lot about copywriting, you've gone through the Copywriting Module, you've seen some of the highest performing copy pieces in the world, you've gone through this particular module, and you've gone through the sales funnel module, you've explored copy again, copy is everything, right? We've spoken about how important writing compelling sales pieces is. This is no different, so you're just writing a really cool script and you're giving it to a voiceover artist, and of course the script is gonna send people to the URL, which is of course the first page of your perfect SLM sales funnel, and then your job with respect to actually the setting up the campaign is done. All that's left of course, is you need to hire the best talent, okay? I don't want you guys speaking in the radio yourselves. I know it's been done successfully before, I know there are a lot of CEOs who are the face of their particular brand, and they like to speak about whatever it is they so will do, but I'm urging you guys, especially when you're running your first test campaigns on radio to actually just hire some voice talent, it's so cheap, it's so easy. I'll show you guys how to do it now, and once you guys actually have a script, you just get that script, give it to the voice talent, they'll record it, give you back an MP3 file, you just give that MP3 file to the radio station, and you're done. Obviously you need to speak to them about your targeting, and your targeting guys should be spot on by now, you guys have a good idea of your existing buyers, you've data mined through, towered data, you've gone through Alexa, you've gone through Quantcast, you looked at your competitors, you've gone through the Facebook audiences, you understand exactly who it is that you're selling to. Now that you know that, targeting becomes really easy, you just explain your targeting to the radio network, and you give them the MP3 file, and you're done. So when you're actually developing the video, or excuse me, the MP3, the voice, just go into Fiber against Fiber.com, write voiceover, or voiceover talent, or voiceover artist, or whatever, and then you're going to be really spoke for choice, and you can see that for about 60 second voice commercial, you'll pay about $6.98, that's $7 for 60 seconds, for some of the talent here, alternatively you might get a little bit more expensive, if you know the 200 words for $7, you know if you have a two or three minutes advertisement, you can expect to pay at the very most $30, you know $30, and you have a professional voiceover artist reading out your script, making it sound like a million bucks, and you can even ask this particular voiceover artist to put some music over the script if you like, and they'll find some royalty free music, or they'll tell you, okay, I've found this particular piece of music is really good, but it costs a little bit of money, it's not free, and they might tell you, okay, do you want to consider it, and you have listened to the music, it really sounds really great with his or her voice, you might be prepared to pay $15 or $20 for that music to play in the background, and that is it, then you're done, then your commercial is done, it's just a matter of giving it to the radio station, and you're off to the races, couldn't be easier, right? Okay, so just to wrap things up for this particular module, I want to speak briefly about online to offline. Now, the reason I'm only speaking about it briefly is because you guys have an idea of how it works, we spoke about that dentist example, right, where you're actually generating the lead online, and then offline the dentist is actually, we spoke about it, and the dentist is actually sending the prospect down the funnel by encouraging them for either free clean, which was done online, and then the teeth whitening them, the veneers and so forth, and they ran them down the sales funnel, and that is really how it works. The only way I've seen online to offline work really effectively outside of a standard lead gen opt-in form that encourages visitors to go to actual physical store and redeem their coupon or their offer or whatever it is that they got for free online is to actually rely on telemarketing. That's the only other way really. You know, so the only other way that I've seen with success, I can probably rattle off another 20, 30 different marketing strategies that we've tried with respect to online to offline, but the most effective was actually degenerating leads online. Remember how we spoke about it in our first module, the difference between an opt-in page and a lead gen page, they're virtually the same thing, except a lead generation page, excuse me, just has a few more elements on the page, and one of the elements, for example, is a telephone number and the other is a physical address sometimes, sometimes it is, sometimes it isn't, but what I've noticed is asking for a telephone number is very useful because it could easily be followed up with a awesome sales call, and that sales call, of course, would need to be 100% guided by the straight line persuasion system, that's the SLP system, not the SLM system. If you're trying to sell anything over the phone with any other kind of system or sales skills, stop immediately, I promise you, if you're throwing money away, the SLP system is by far the single best telephone sales system in the world bar none. So what I've seen a lot of success with is people running online campaigns to generate leads, and then they'll take those leads, pass it on to a kick ass sales team who are trained in SLP, so SLP professionals and pros, you know, experts, who will then get on the phone and then try to convert higher ticket sales, because if you can't really sell things for more than $2,000 to $5,000 online, it gets really challenging. Anything above $2,000 to $5,000, say $5,000 at the very most, then you're really going to need to go offline. You need to have a conversation with somebody, whether it's over the phone or face to face, but a conversation with a real person typically needs to take place for the real higher ticket sales, and that's where I see the online to offline working really, really well for those high ticket sales. It works brilliantly. But to actually go online, for example, and then there were a lead gen page, excuse me, and the visitor online fills in the lead gen page and then puts their physical address, and then after they put their physical address, they then sent a direct mail piece, for example, a 3D mail or a postcard or something. Unfortunately, I haven't seen it work. You know, I've tried it, I've tried it, and it doesn't work, and we stopped doing it because it doesn't work, and I don't want to teach you guys anything that may sound good in theory, but hasn't been tested to be wildly successful in practice. And right now, if you want to go online to offline, which is awesome, online to offline is one of my favorite strategies. I can't stress enough. The reason it's one of my favorite strategies is because every time a sale comes in, it is a huge sale because you're actually speaking directly to the customer and you're actually having a conversation with the customer, and that conversation is guided by the straight line persuasion system, which is wildly successful. Like the conversion rates are phenomenal. You're able to close anybody who's closeable. SLM is built around that system, and SLP is the system. That's the genesis. If you're following the SLP approach to making sales online and controlling sales online, excuse me, offline, then you're going to convert like gangbusters, and each single time you convert because it's a face-to-face or a person-to-person, I should say, sales environment or sales core, then more often than not, the actual value of whatever it is that the salesperson has sold your prospect or customer is usually really, really high, which means that customer is worth so, so much, to us, and more often than not, it leads to wildly successful campaigns. So I love online-to-offline, but when you think online-to-offline, think telephone, which is why you see a telephone right in front of you. Alternatively, think face-to-face. If you're doing house calls for whatever it is you sell, great strategy is, of course, online-to-offline, where you generate the lead online. An example is real estate agents. They can generate leads online. They could send people to a lead gen page that tells them the value of their home without them having to leave the house based on the most recent market data and the recent sale prices. And people say, oh, well, I'm interested to know how much my home is worth. They're feeling that lead gen form. And that lead gen form obviously has their telephone number and address, and then the salesperson will use that information to contact the particular or prospective home vendor or home owner who's prepared or thinking about selling his home, and then try to organize an opportunity to meet with them face-to-face to get that listing. So you can see the functionality of the straight-line marketing system, how it works both offline-to-online, online-to-offline, online-to-online.
